title: Research Technology Core, College of Health Solutions

summary.hook: This poster describes the Research Technology Core (RTC) at ASU’s College of Health Solutions and how it supports health research through custom mobile apps, participant-management web apps, and integrated wearable/IoT platforms.
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes the Research Technology Core (RTC) at ASU’s College of Health Solutions and how it supports health research through custom mobile apps, participant-management web apps, and integrated wearable/IoT platforms. The RTC emphasizes minimally invasive, at-home participation, real-time feedback, and scalable systems that remain compliant with HIPAA and FDA standards. The poster highlights example solutions (walkability app, sleep/CPAP reports, screen-time behavior tools, Fitbit + Shelly plug integration) and reports cumulative achievements: 10,000+ participants served, 66 projects deployed, and 5 funded projects in the pipeline.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Create systems that enhance outcomes, streamline processes, and advance health research for researchers and participants.

Approach: Population: “Researchers and participants” served through multiple studies; total participants served reported cumulatively. Primary outcome: Not stated on poster (program impact described qualitatively + operational metrics).
Finding: RTC supports at-home, minimally invasive participant engagement via mobile apps and wearable/IoT integrations (Fitbit, CPAP, glucose monitors, Shelly plug).

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Mobile applications integrated with wearable and IoT technologies provide transformative advantages for health research by enabling real-time data collection, personalized feedback, and improved engagement; these tools monitor behaviors in natural settings and reduce barriers to participation, yielding actionable insights into health behaviors, interventions, and outcomes. Implications (poster-grounded): Researchers can bridge research ideas to real-world health improvements using scalable participant-facing apps plus back-end management tools. At-home participation and real-time feedback may improve adherence and retention and improve the accuracy of behavior measurement in naturalistic settings. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Real-time monitoring + personalized feedback + reminders/education and reduced participation barriers foster long-term adherence and behavior change. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Not stated on poster
key_numbers: Program totals: 10,000+ participants served; 66 projects deployed; 5 funded projects in pipeline.
The poster highlights example solutions (walkability app, sleep/CPAP reports, screen-time behavior tools, Fitbit + Shelly plug integration) and reports cumulative achievements:...
takeaways: RTC supports at-home, minimally invasive participant engagement via mobile apps and wearable/IoT integrations (Fitbit, CPAP, glucose monitors, Shelly plug).

The poster includes multiple example product screenshots illustrating: curated education + sleep/diet tracking; participant study oversight dashboards; screen-time usage visualization; neighborhood walkability discovery; sleep/CPAP report visualizations; connected Fitbit + Shelly plug monitoring.

Authors’ stated conclusions: Mobile applications integrated with wearable and IoT technologies provide transformative advantages for health research by enabling real-time data collection, personalized feedback, and improved engagement; these tools monitor behaviors in natural settings and reduce barriers to participation, yielding actionable insights into health behaviors, interventions, and outcomes.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: Over what time period were 10,000+ participants served and 66 projects deployed?

what_to_ask.question: What kinds of projects are included in the “66 projects deployed,” and what are the most common use cases (sleep, physical activity, diet, adherence)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How do you validate that data streamed from devices is accurate and complete (missingness detection, calibration checks)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: For a PI planning a grant, what’s the best way to scope an RTC request to strengthen feasibility and innovation claims?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
